insights: IT department exhibits an exceptionally high expense rejection rate of 44.19%, which is 34.99 percentage points above the organizational average of 9.20% and nearly 5x the rate of the largest department (Customer Support at 5.99%).

IT’s elevated rejection rate persists across all expense categories—such as Assets (56.52% vs 4.85% in Customer Support) and Travel (30.00% vs 10.91%)—indicating the driver is not category-specific submission behavior but department-wide evaluation practices.

IT’s rejection rate is not explained by expense amount: its mean ($4,318) and median ($4,132) are lower than non-IT submissions, yet rejection rates remain significantly elevated across all amount bins (25–83% vs 6.73–13.04% overall).

IT’s rejection rate shows a clear worsening temporal trend—rising from 35.71% in 2023 to 60.00% in 2024—with early 2024 peaks (February: 83.33%, March: 66.67%), suggesting a recent policy shift or change in approver behavior rather than stable operational variance.

Department-specific approval standards are the primary driver of IT’s outlier rejection rate, as evidenced by consistent elevation across categories, amounts, and time—ruling out submission composition, financial scale, or random variation as explanatory factors.

Product Management has a 0% rejection rate (0/12), while Customer Support accounts for over half of all submissions (267/500) yet maintains the lowest rejection rate among large departments—highlighting stark inter-departmental divergence in approval discipline and risk tolerance.
summary: The core driver of varying expense rejection rates across departments is department-specific approval standards—not differences in expense type, amount, or timing—with IT department representing a critical outlier: its 44.19% rejection rate is both statistically and operationally anomalous, having worsened sharply to 60.00% in 2024 and persisting across all categories and amount ranges. This signals inconsistent application of financial controls, likely due to recent changes in IT’s approval policy or personnel, posing risks of delayed reimbursements, employee friction, and misaligned budget execution. In contrast, departments like Product Management (0% rejections) and Customer Support (5.99%, highest volume) demonstrate disciplined, scalable approval practices. To improve financial management, immediate focus should be placed on auditing IT’s approval criteria, comparing them against cross-departmental benchmarks, and aligning governance standards—particularly given the accelerating trend observed in early 2024.
